coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to investigate the application potential of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized with D-mannose as a carbon source precursor and its nitrogen-doped derivatives (GCDs and GLCDs) in plant growth. GCDs and GLCDs were successfully synthesized using hydrothermal methods, and their morphology, structure, and optical properties were detailed. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) showed that the prepared CDs had uniform spherical nanostructures with particle sizes of 2.63 nm and 1.22 nm, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis revealed that the surface of CDs was rich in functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and nitrogen functional groups introduced by nitrogen doping. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectra showed that GCDs and GLCDs had high quantum yields of 2.39 % and 6.60 %, respectively. In plant growth experiments, GCDs and GLCDs significantly affected the growth of mung bean sprouts, especially at a concentration of 0.08 mg/mL. The increase in chlorophyll content suggests that CDs may promote plant growth by increasing the efficiency of photosynthesis. The results show that D-mannose-derived carbon dots have great potential for application as a new type of plant growth regulator.

head.ce:title: D-Mannose-derived carbon dots and their application to plant growth

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to investigate the application potential of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized with D-mannose as a carbon source precursor and its nitrogen-doped derivatives (GCDs and GLCDs) in plant growth. GCDs and GLCDs were successfully synthesized using hydrothermal methods, and their morphology, structure, and optical properties were detailed. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) showed that the prepared CDs had uniform spherical nanostructures with particle sizes of 2.63 nm and 1.22 nm, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis revealed that the surface of CDs was rich in functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and nitrogen functional groups introduced by nitrogen doping. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectra showed that GCDs and GLCDs had high quantum yields of 2.39 % and 6.60 %, respectively. In plant growth experiments, GCDs and GLCDs significantly affected the growth of mung bean sprouts, especially at a concentration of 0.08 mg/mL. The increase in chlorophyll content suggests that CDs may promote plant growth by increasing the efficiency of photosynthesis. The results show that D-mannose-derived carbon dots have great potential for application as a new type of plant growth regulator.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: With a growing global population and evolving lifestyles, there is an increasing consumer demand for healthy eating, making fruits and vegetables an integral part of our daily diet. However, the twin threats of population growth and global warming pose serious challenges to vegetable and fruit producers, who must increase crop yields while reducing negative environmental impacts in a changing environment [1,2]. Food production relies heavily on the heavy use of pesticides and fertilizers, but this has failed to break through the bottleneck of increasing crop yields and has caused severe damage to the environment, ecological balance, and biodiversity. Therefore, developing environmentally friendly and efficient crop productivity improvement strategies has become a significant challenge in agricultural science [3].

Photosynthesis is fundamental to plant growth, and it produces more than 90 % of crop biomass by capturing solar radiation and converting it into chemical energy, driving the conversion of carbon dioxide into carbohydrates [4]. Despite this, the photosynthetic efficiency of crops is usually much lower than the theoretical maximum due to the limitations of solar energy use efficiency. The limited absorption capacity of photosynthetic pigments and the narrow spectral coverage are the main obstacles that limit the improvement of photosynthesis efficiency [5,6]. To overcome these limitations, scientists have been exploring new materials and technologies that can improve the efficiency of photosynthesis. Photosynthesis by plants through chloroplasts converts solar energy into chemical energy, and light with wavelengths from ultraviolet to 800 nm plays an essential role in the morphological structure of plants [7,8]. However, the light plants used for photosynthesis are mainly in the blue and red regions below 700 nm, as these wavelengths match the wavelengths of the light absorbed by chloroplasts [9]. Therefore, increasing the conversion rate of solar energy and maximizing the use of solar energy is a top priority in current research [3]. In addition, as an essential part of the environment, plants play a vital role in maintaining ecosystem balance and providing food sources for humans and animals. Therefore, understanding the effects of nanomaterials on plant growth is of great significance to improve crop yield and quality.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their excellent ultraviolet absorption, coordination fluorescence, high light stability, good biocompatibility, and low cost [10–12]. The carbon dots also retain light absorption near the ultraviolet ray, with peaks concentrated in the 260–320 nm wavelength range and tails extending into the visible region [13]. These properties make CDs an ideal candidate for improving the efficiency of plant photosynthesis. In particular, the photoluminescence properties of CDs can increase the photosynthetic activity of plants by converting or transferring energy to chloroplasts, thereby enhancing light capture and energy transfer in plant photosynthesis systems [14–16]. In recent years, in the context of the circular economy and reducing the earth's resources, waste should be converted into value-added materials. Attention has shifted to using biomass materials to prepare CDs and lignocellulosic biomass, as many researchers have favoured the most abundant renewable natural resource. Many studies have explored the production of CDs from the components in lignocellulosic biomass, such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin [17,18]. In this study, we used a hydrothermal method to synthesize CDs. The hydrothermal method is more suitable for preparing CDs based on natural products due to its low cost, environmental protection, simple operation, adequate reaction, and relatively uniform particle size of the prepared CDs [19]. However, the complex interaction of lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose during the hydrothermal degradation of lignocellulose leads to uncertainty in the hydrothermal synthesis of lignocellulose-based CDs, which makes it challenging to identify the specific formation mechanism of lignocellulosic CDs [20]. In addition, some researchers have found that hemicellulose and lignin are the main degradation components during hydrothermal degradation, while the degradation of cellulose is complex [21,22]. The molecular chain of hemicellulose is composed of pyranose and furanose units, and D-mannose, as one of the main monosaccharides of hemicellulose, is composed of a central backbone and carboxyl group, has a high carbon content, and is a good source for the preparation of carbon dots. The presence of functional groups on the surface also makes it suitable for various surface functionalizations, providing the possibility of functionalization of carbon dots [23–25].

In this study, the undoped D-mannose carbon dots (GCDs) and nitrogen-doped D-mannose carbon dots (GLCDs) were firstly synthesized by hydrothermal method, using D-mannose as carbon precursor and o-phenylenediamine as nitrogen dopant. The surface morphology, chemical structure and optical properties of the two D-mannose derived carbon dots were comparatively studied, and the hydrothermal synthesis mechanism and CDs’ fluorescence mechanism were explored. Then, the obtained D-mannose-derived carbon dots were used for the growth of mung bean sprouts to explore the feasibility of using D-mannose-derived carbon points to promote plant growth. It is expected that this study can provide a new way for the high-value utilization of D-mannose.

D-mannose (99 %), o-phenylenediamine (AR, 98 %), and quinine sulfate (98 %) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), Acetone and Ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd(Nanjing, China), and purified water with a conductivity of 1.08μS/cm was obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Mung beans are purchased from a local supermarket (Nanjing, China). All reagents were used without further purification. Purified water was used through-out the experiment.

The D-mannose-derived carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis[26], and the preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 . Specifically, D-mannose (0.180 g) was added to purified water (50 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 10 min to obtain the initial reaction solution, which was transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) equipped with PTFE for hydrothermal reaction. The hydrothermal reaction temperature and duration were 160 °C and 6 h, respectively. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was poured out from the autoclave. To remove unreacted impurities in the mixture, it was filtered with microporous membrane (pore size, 0.22 μm) and centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 10 min). And then the supernatant obtained by centrifugal treatment was subjected to dialysis (1000 Da, 48 h). The remaining solution is undoped D-mannose carbon dots (GCDs) solution. Solid GCDs for subsequent characterization was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying at − 65 °C for 72 h.

Nitrogen-doped D-mannose carbon dots (GLCDs) solution and its solid powder were prepared by the same method. The difference is that its initial reaction solution contains nitrogen dopant, o-phenylenediamine (0.108 g).

The pictures of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of the D-mannose-derived CQDs solution was obtained on a JEM-2100UHR microscope (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV using ultra-thin carbon film-coated copper grids.

The functional groups on the surface of D-Mannose-derived CDs was characterized by a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (VERTEX80V, Bruker, Germany) using KBr pellet technique. The test mode was “Smart iTR diamond ATR”, and the spectra scanning range and the resolutions were 4000–500 cm−1 and 4 cm−1, respectively.

The D-Mannose-derived CDs' elemental composition and bonding states were analyzed using a X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (AXIS-Ultra DLD, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), and the binding energy was calibrated based on the C 1 s peak at 284.6 eV. X-ray diffraction(XRD)(XRD Ultima IV, Japan) and Raman spectroscopy(DXR532, America) were used to analyze the crystal structure and graphitization structure of GCDs and GLCDs, where the XRD range was 5°-40° in the 2θ range; the excitation wavelength of Raman spectroscopy was set to 780 nm, the slit width was 50 μm, and the test range was 1000–2000 cm−1.

The optical properties of the CQDs were assessed by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, photoluminescence fluorescence (PL) spectrum, as well as photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of ten-fold diluted CDs solution were recorded by an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 950, America). The excitation-emission spectra of ten-fold diluted CDs solution were measured by a transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, Fluoio Max-4, America), and the slit width of excitation and emission were set as 1 nm and 1 nm.

The potential of the D-Mannose-derived CDs solution was determined by a Zeta potential potentiometer (Malvern Zetasizer Pro, Britain). The measurement of zeta potential for each sample was performed 3 times at 25 ℃, and the average value was used for discussion.

Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of the obtained GCDs and GLCDs at optimized excitation wavelength was studied using a transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, Fluoio Max-4, USA). The obtained lifetime value was fitted well via a double-exponential function as shown in Equation (1), and the average lifetime was calculated according to equation (2)[27],

Quantum yield (QY) is the number of emitted photons relative to the number of absorbed photons. The QY of the synthesized CDs was tested according to an established relative method [28]. Here, quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as the standard reference (QY = 54 %, excited at 360 nm) to calculate the QY of CDs in purified water. All absorbance values were controlled under 0.1 with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (kex = 360 nm) to avoid re-absorption, and all the FL spectra in QY determination were obtained by exciting at 360 nm with an excitation slit a 2.5 nm and 10 nm, respectively. The relative QY of the CDs was calculated by equation (3).

The cultivation method of mung bean seedlings was improved on the method of Xu et al.[29]. Firstly, mung beans of similar size were selected from the same batch of mung beans purchased as test seeds and soaked in purified water for 6 h and then soaked in ethanol solution (75 %) for 5 min. Then, 100 mL petri dishes were taken as containers for cultivating mung bean sprouts, and 10 samples were prepared according to Table 1 . Among them, the CDs’ concentration of Nos. 1–3, 4–6 and 7–9 was 0.02 mg/mL, 0.08 mg/mL and 0.32 mg/mL, respectively, and no CDs solution was added to No. 10 as Control. The amount of purified water, CDs solution and mung bean seedlings in each surface dish is 15 mL,15 mL and 20. Finally, all the surface dishes were placed in a constant temperature culture chamber (temperature 22 °C, humidity 65 %, light 20000Lux) for 10 days. The relevant values of mung bean sprout growth were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical parameter measurement of mung bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: The main physical parameters of mung bean sprouts include root length, stem length, leaf length, dry weight and fresh weight.

To measure the root length, stem length, and leaf length of the sprouts, lay the sprouts flat on a piece of cardboard and take photos for comparison. Leaf length is measured directly with a ruler. In order to measure the length of the bent root and stem of bean sprout, a string is needed, i.e. the string is first placed along the bending trend of the root and stem, and then the length of the rope is measured.

The dry weight and fresh weight of bean sprouts were measured by an analytical balance (Electronic balance, 2011F145-11, Saidolis Science Instruments (Beijing, China) Co., Ltd.) and an oven (Electric heating constant temperature drying box, 101-1B, Shanghai Qiuzuo Technology Instrument Co., Ltd.). Firstly, the fresh weight of bean sprouts was measured directly by the analytical balance, and then they were placed in the oven, dried at 105 °C for 5 min, then at 75 °C to a constant weight, and then its dry weight was measured by the analytical balance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of chlorophyll content in mung bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: Chlorophyll is an important nutrient index of mung bean sprouts. The chlorophyll content in Mung bean sprouts was measured referencing method[30,31]. Firstly, fresh bean sprout leaves (5 g) was cut into pieces, which was put into a mortar (50 mL),with 10 mL extracting agent (Acetone: anhydrous ethanol = 1:1). The mixture was ground thoroughly until it turned dark green, then was transferred to a conical flask (100 mL) and heated in a water bath at 65 °C for 15 min until the solid in the mixture turned brown. Then, the brown mixture was centrifuged (Medical centrifuge, TG16-WS, Changsha Weierkangxiangying Centrifuge Co., Ltd.) at 4000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 663 nm and 645 nm, respectively.

The chlorophyll content in mung bean sprouts is calculated by formula 4, 5, and 6 [30].

Data analysis was performed using IBM SPSS Statistics 27, and every treatment was repeated at least 3 times. The statistical significance of all data was determined using One-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) and compared using Duncan test at the level of p < 0.05 using SPSS 27. In all graphs, error bars represent the standard error of the mean.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of D-mannose-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 provides the morphology, particle size and distribution of the obtained D-mannose-derived CDs. For GCDs and GLCDs, the average particle size is 2.63 nm and 1.22 nm, respectively, and the particle size distribution is 1.6 ∼ 3 nm and 0.6 ∼ 2.2 nm, respectively. Two CDs samples appear uniform spherical nanostructures. These nanoparticles are evenly distributed in water without multiple nuclei embedded. Uniform morphology and deep carbonization CDs samples is due to carbon chains’ growth into nuclei by continuous polymerization. It suggests the presence of organic groups on a single carbon sphere surface[32].

Moreover, both CDs samples show high crystallinity with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2b and e), which corresponds to the (100 facets) lattice plane of graphene [26,33]. It demonstrates that the carbon core of CDs possesses graphene-like crystal structure. These two carbon quantum dots contain a large number of graphitized sp2 carbon structures in their carbon cores. In short, the particle size of both CDs is nanometer (<10 nm), which is easily absorbed by plant roots[34].
XPS, FT-IR, XRD and Raman was used to determine the chemical and surface composition of, synthesized D-mannose-derived CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 .

Fig. 3a shows the XPS full-scan spectrum of the prepared GCDs and GLCDs. At 284 eV and 530 eV, the spectrum has an obvious absorption peak, belonging to C1s and O1s, respectively [35]. From the XPS full-scan spectrum of GLCDs, it has three distinct absorption peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV and 531.6 eV, which is ascribed to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively [28,36]. In the high-resolution spectra of C1s of GCDs and GLCDs (shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. 3d), the three peaks at 283.1 eV, 284.7 eV-286 eV and 288 eV correspond to the vibration of C–H, C–C/C=C and C=O, respectively [26,37,38]. Fig. 3c and Fig. 3e give the high-resolution spectra of O1s of GCDs and GLCDs. Both spectra have two absorption peaks at 531.1 eV/532.2 eV and 531.5 eV/532.7 eV, respectively, which is attributed to the vibration of C=O and C-O [36]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s of GLCDs (shown in Fig. 3g) have two absorption peaks at 398.9 eV and 400.6 eV, respectively, corresponding to the vibration of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N [35].

The above XPS results of GCDs and GLCDs can be further validated by studying the their FT-IR spectra (shown in Fig. 3g)., There are two absorption peaks on the spectra of GCDs, located at 3346 cm−1 and 1037 cm−1, respectively, which are attributed to the tensile vibration of –OH and the telescopic vibration of C-O [34,39]. For the spectra of GLCDs, the peaks at 3278 cm−1, 1738 cm−1, 1635 cm-1and 1330 cm−1 are ascribed to the tensile vibrations of –NH2, C=O, C=N and C-N groups [28,40,41]. The FT-IR spectra of D-mannose, the precursor of carbon source, are also shown in Fig. 3g. Moreover, all spectra of the three samples has a typical tensile vibration peak at 2916 cm−1, attributed to the tensile vibration of C–H [26]. Fig. 3h shows the XRD spectra of GCDs and GLCDs. The spectral peaks of both CD samples are located at 18.9°, corresponding to the [100] plane of graphene[42] GCDs show a broader diffraction peak at 2θ ≈ 18.9°, indicating that they have a graphitized structure, but the wider peak means that the degree of graphitization is lower and the crystal structure is not completely ordered. In addition, GLCDs also have a diffraction peak at 2θ ≈ 26°, corresponding to the [002] plane of graphene[28]. Its appearance indicates that the interlayer stacking of GLCDs is very close to graphite and indicates that in the core of GLCDs, highly conjugated sp2 domains dominate, which may be related to the structural changes caused by nitrogen doping. Nitrogen doping may change the arrangement of carbon atoms and promote the growth of the [100] plane, thereby enhancing the order of the material[43,44]. Overall, the XRD patterns of GLCDs show a significant effect of nitrogen doping on the crystal structure of the material, which leads to changes in the stacking of graphene layers and improves the orderliness of the crystal. In the Raman spectra shown in Fig. 3i, both GCDs and GLCDs exhibit peaks around 1330 cm-1 and 1636 cm-1, corresponding to the D band and G band, respectively. The D band is associated with the amorphous sp3 carbon structure, while the G band arises from the graphitic sp2 carbon structure[45]. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band, denoted as ID/IG, provides insight into the degree of crystallinity: a higher ID/IG ratio indicates lower crystallinity, and vice versa. The ID/IG values for GCDs and GLCDs were calculated to be 0.67 and 0.63, respectively. This suggests that in both GCDs and GLCDs, the graphitic sp2 carbon structure dominates, while the amorphous sp3 carbon structure is less prevalent. However, the graphitization degree and crystallinity of GLCDs are slightly higher compared to GCDs. This corresponds with the structure of XRD.

In a word, the above XPS, FTIR, XRD and Raman results showed that GCDs were rich in C and O, while nitrogen-doped GLCDs were rich in C, N and O, and had functional groups such as C–C/C=C, C-O, –NH2, C=O, C-N, O–H, C=N, etc. Both GCDs and GLCDs have a large number of oxygen-containing groups on the surface, so they have good water dispersibility, and the abundant surface functional groups are conducive to the entry of carbon dots into plant cells and their functions[42,46].

Photoluminescence (PL) emission behavior is a characteristic optical performance of CDs, directly affecting their application in different fields. The three-dimensional excitation-emission spectra of GCDs and GLCDs are shown in Fig. 4 .
Fig. 4a shows the fluorescence intensity of GCDs in the in the emission wavelength of 400–550 nm when the emission wavelength changes from 320 nm to 420 nm.

The high-intensity fluorescence emission is located in the region with excitation wavelength of about 360 nm and emission wavelength emission wavelength of about 450 nm (red). As shown in Fig. 4b., compared with undoped CDs, the emission wavelength of nitrogen-doped D-mannose CDs is redshifted from 450 nm (blue region) to 504 nm (blue and green region). In addition, with the increase of excitation wavelength (from 400 to 600 nm), the emission center position of GLCDs gradually shifts from 465 nm (blue) to 504 nm (blue-green), showing a typical excitation-dependent behavior. It indicates that the nitrogen doping process for GLCDs changes the original emission behavior, and realizes energy band regulation by introducing new energy levels. It may be attributed to its surface functional groups, according to the results of XPS and infrared, it can be seen that GLCDs contain functional groups such as N–H, C-N, C=N, etc, and N elements are successfully incorporated into CDs, while nitrogen doping will change the electronic structure of CDs, especially their band structure. This is because nitrogen atoms have high electronegativity, and when incorporated into CDs, they may affect their light absorption and light emission properties by changing the electron density distribution or electronic density of states of CDs. This change in electronic structure results in a smaller energy difference between the excited and ground states of the CDs, resulting in a redshift in the wavelength of fluorescence emission.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GCDs and GLCDs are shown in Fig. 4c and d. As shown in Fig. 4c and 4d, when exposed to ultraviolet light (365 nm), GCDs emits bright blue light, while GLCDs appears bright blue-green light, which again indicates that the original emission behavior of the CDs is changed through the nitrogen doping process. In Fig. 4c, there is a weaker shoulder at 231 nm, which is attributed to π → π* electronic transition of the aromatic C=C in the surface function groups of undoped D-mannose-derived CDs. In comparison, the typical absorption band at 285 nm is ascribed to n → π* electronic transition of GCDs’ surface state [47,48]. However, after doping nitrogen, the GLCDs sample presents a different UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 d). The weaker shoulder shifts to 237 nm, which belongs to π → π* electronic transition of GLCDs’ aromatic structure; and the typical absorption peak shifts to 329 nm, which represents n → π* transition of the C=N bond in the structure of GLCDs [49,50]. It shows that nitrogen doping can change the surface state of D-mannose derived CDs.

In addition, the quantum yields of GCDs and GLCDs are calculated according to Equation (3), which are 2.39 % and 6.60 %, respectively. Nitrogen doping introduces new surface defects and electronic states in carbon dots. Nitrogen atoms, due to their electronegativity, create new functional groups like amines (–NH2) and pyridine-like structures, which can improve the excitation and emission processes. These new states may reduce non-radiative energy losses and enhance fluorescence efficiency, leading to a higher quantum yield.

Zeta potential refers to the shear surface potential (also called electrokinetic potential or electrokinetic potential, expressed by ζ) is an important indicator of the stability of colloidal dispersion system. In this study, the stability of CDs in purified water was evaluated by studying the Zeta potential of GCDs and GLCDs, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The surface charge of GCDs and GLCDs is −12.13 mv and −14.58 mv, respectively. The large ζ values of both CDs samples lead to large repulsion between negatively charged CDs, preventing them from polymerizing and providing stability for the water dispersion system of GCDs and GLCDs. Here, the negative values of ζ can be attributed to the functional groups on the surface of GCDs and GLCDs, such as carboxyl group (–COOH), hydroxyl group (–OH), ether bond (−O-), and so on. These functional groups are capable of generating negative charges through dissociation. For example, a carboxyl group may lose a proton (H+) in aqueous solution to form a negative charge (–COO-)[51,52]. It is interesting that the ζ value of GLCDs is higher than that of GCDs, which is due to the doping of N element in GLCDs. The presence of N element optimizes the structure of GLCDs, and increases its electron supply, which promotes the efficient separation of electrons and holes, thereby increases its electron density. This enables its efficient photoconversion and electron donation [53,54]. This is the advantage of nitrogen doped carbon dots.
The time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra of GCDs and GLCDs are shown in Fig. 5b and c, and the attenuation fitting results are listed in Table S1.

The fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots refers to the transition time between the excited state and the ground state in the process of luminescence, and can also be understood as the duration of fluorescence emission. The fast decay curve (τ1) is closely related to the radiative recombination of excitons in the core structure. Due to the non-radiative recombination of photoexcited carriers on the surface, the excited surface state produces a slow fluorescence lifetime (τ2). τ1 and τ2 are associated with the electronic transitions of CDs and belong to the recombination process of the core and surface states, respectively [26,55]. Here, the mean lifetime (τave) of GLCDs is 5.30 ns, which is higher than that of GCDs (2.54 ns). It indicates that the fluorescence properties of nitrogen-doped CDs are jointly controlled by their sp2 conjugated system and surface functional groups. The nitrogen-containing functional groups such as –NH2, C-N in the surface of nitrogen-doped CDs (GLCDs) increases its surface defect density, thus extending its fluorescence lifetime[56–58]. This characterization is consistent with the results of XRD and Raman.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of D-mannose-derived CDs on the growth of mung bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: Mung bean seedlings were cultured with CDs aqueous solution (0–0.32 mg/mL) (experimental group, groups 1–9 in Table 1) and pure water (control group, group 10 in Table 1) as Control. Fig. S1 photos of mung bean seedlings in each group at the end of cultivation on the 4th, 7th and 10th days and Fig. 6 (photos of control sample, mung bean seedlings treated with 0.08 mg/mL D-Mannose、GCDs and GLCDs) indicate that D-mannose-derived CDs can indeed improve the overall growth of mung bean seedlings. This may be because the surface of D-mannose-derived carbon dots is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups and hydrophilic groups(–OH、–COOH、–NH2), which promotes the absorption of water and nutrients by plants. The root system is the main part of plants absorb nutrients and water. By improving the absorption capacity of the root system, carbon dots can provide plants with richer nutrients, thereby promoting the growth of bean sprouts[59]. The carbon dots may also improve the efficiency of plant photosynthesis through their own fluorescence emission performance, increase the photosynthetic pigment content in plant leaves, thereby enhancing photosynthesis. This provides more energy for the plant to support its growth[60,61]. Another very interesting experimental phenomenon is that the promotion effect of CDs on the growth of mung bean seedlings is also related to its concentration. The growth of mung beans (No. 4–6) treated with 0.08 mg/mL is better than that of samples No. 1–3 (CDs concentration is 0.02 mg/mL). However, when the concentration of CDs is increased to 0.32 mg/mL, this promoting effect is significantly weakened. Therefore, when the D-mannose-derived CDs is used to promote the germination and growth of mung beans, there is a threshold of its concentration, that is, 0.08 mg/mL. When the concentration of CDs is lower or higher than the threshold, the promoting effect is not ideal. When carbon dot solution promotes the growth of bean sprouts, too low concentrations of carbon dots may not provide enough biological activity to promote plant growth, while too high concentrations may lead to oxidative stress or metabolic imbalance, which will instead inhibit the normal growth of plants. Carbon dots at a concentration of 0.08 mg/mL can effectively promote plant growth, regulate hormone levels, and improve nutrient absorption, but when they exceed or below 0.08 mg/mL, they may have a negative impact on the plant. Therefore, 0.08 mg/mL is the most suitable carbon dot concentration[62,63].

In addition, the promoting effect of undoped and nitrogen-doped mannose CDs on mung bean growth is also different. As shown in the growth day comparison pictures in Fig. S1 and the data of mung bean seedlings cultured in different concentrations of carbon dot solutions in Fig. S2, the promoting effect of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (GLCDs) is better than that of undoped carbon dots (GCDs).the promoting effect of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (GLCDs) is better than that of undoped carbon dots (GCDs). CDs can improve the separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons and hole pairs in photosynthesis, increase the number of excitons, and expand light capture. Compared with GCDs, GLCDs contain N elements, which can increase electron density and reduce the recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs, and then act as electron donors to donate electrons to the photosystem[64]. The functional groups of –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 contained in carbon dots contain lone pairs, and the excess of these electrons will affect the separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons and hole pairs during photosynthesis, thereby increasing the number of excitons and expanding light energy harvesting, thereby promoting plant growth[63,64].

In order to further study the promotion effect of carbon dots on the growth of mung bean seedlings, we respectively measured the root, stem and leaf length, as well as the weight and the chlorophyll content of mung bean seedlings cultivated for 7 days, as shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a, b and c are the histograms of root length, stem length and leaf length of mung bean seedlings treated with 0.08 mg/mL concentration of GCDs and GLCDs. The promotion effect of both CDs on the growth of mung bean sprouts is mainly reflected in the roots and leaves. For example, the root length of mung bean sprouts treated with 0.08 mg/mL GCDs and GLCDs increases by 106.45 % and 51.13 %, and the leaf length increases by 24.76 % and 7.52 %, respectively. However, the increase in stem length of mung bean sprouts treated with GCDs and GLCDs was not statistically significant compared with the control group, indicating that both CDs can effectively promote the growth of the roots and leaves of mung bean seedlings. Fig. 7d and 7e show the fresh and dry weight of mung bean seedlings in three groups (the control group, and two groups treated with 0.08 mg/mL GCDs and GLCDs, respectively). Compared with the control group, the fresh weight and dry weight of mung bean seedlings treated with GCDs increases by 33.20 % and 29.51 %, respectively, and treated with GLCDs increases by 66.43 % and 70.71 %, respectively. So, from the perspective of biomass accumulation, these two CDs have a good effect on the growth of mung beans.

Fig. 7f shows the chlorophyll content of mung bean seedlings treated with 0.08 mg/mL concentration of GCDs and GLCDs. Compared with the control group, the contents of chlorophyll-a, chlorophyll-b and total chlorophyll in mung bean seedlings cultured with GCDs or GLCDs treatment are increased. This is because GCDs and GLCDs can serve as both electron donors and acceptors, thereby facilitating energy transfer to chloroplasts and regulating electron transport within the photosynthetic system. As electron donors, CDs efficiently capture and transfer electrons due to their high surface area and electron-rich structure[65]. Additionally, CDs function as electron acceptors within the electron transport chain, enhancing its conductivity, improving electron transport efficiency, and promoting electron exchange between photosystems I and II. This process supports ATP production, oxygen generation, and the reduction cycles involved in water splitting and the conversion of NADP to NADPH[66,67].

In particular, the increase of chlorophyll in seedlings treated with GLCDs is more obvious. Chlorophyll is a pigment that captures light energy and converts it into chemical energy through photosynthesis. GLCDs contain nitrogen, which plays an important role in photosynthesis and is an important chemical component of chlorophyll. Doping N element in the CDs can promote the formation of chlorophyll and improve its photosynthetic activity, thus enhancing the photosynthesis of plants [30,68]. In conclusion, mung bean seedlings cultured with 0.08 mg/mL GCDs and GLCDs showed better growth compared to mung bean seedlings cultured in pure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL mechanism of D-mannose-derived CDs and its mechanism in improvement of mung bean sprout growth

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence mechanism of CDs is very complex. At present, there are two recognized PL mechanisms of CDs, namely core state emission caused by the quantum confinement effect and surface state emission caused by surface defects [69].

Considering that there is little difference in the particle size between GCDs and GLCDs, it can be considered that the luminescence of D-mannose derived CDs is not caused by the size-dependent quantum confinement effect; its luminescence behavior is mainly affected by its surface state, especially surface defects and surface functional groups.

Surface defects are usually caused by surface oxidation and can act as a trapping center for excitons, resulting in fluorescence emission. The presence of different surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH), amino (–NH2), and carbonyl (−C=O) groups, results in different electronic states [11].

As shown in Fig. 8 , for GCDs (Fig. 8a), the carbon precursor, D-mannose, mainly undergoes cleavage of glycosidic bonds under high-temperature hydrolysis. That is, glycosidic bonds (1,4-β-glycosidic bonds) are attached to monosaccharide molecules, and are broken by water molecules, resulting in the decomposition of mannose into furanose, pyranose or other small molecule substances [70,71]. In the hydrothermal carbonization process, these decomposition products undergo several stages of change, such as dehydration, polycondensation, polymerization, carbonization or aromatization [19] to form numerous carbon skeletons and GCDs with complex surface structure rich surface functional groups (as shown in Fig. 3) and fluorescence property (as shown in Fig. 5).

Compared with undoped D-mannose carbon dots (GCDs), the nitrogen dopant, o-phenylenediamine, imparts new functional groups (such as C=N bond to nitrogen-doped carbon dots (GLCDs). O-phenylenediamine undergoes deamination or dehydrogenation at high temperatures and pressur to produce benzene, 2,3-diaminophenazine, or other small molecules. O-phenylenediamine can also react directly with water molecules to form hydrates or other hydrolysates [72,73]. These compounds and those produced by D-mannose at high temperature and pressure are further dehydrated intermolecular/intramolecular during hydrothermal treatment to form polymer-like macromolecules, and finally carbonized to form GLCDs, as shown in Fig. 8b. The change in the fluorescence center of GLCDs is attributed to the introduction of the C=N bond, which leads to the reconstitution and expansion of the π conjugation domain in the carbon core, and ultimately to the redshift (from the fluorescence emission center blue for GCDs to blue-green for GLCDs).
In short, GCDs and GLCDs contain various surface groups with different contents. Each has a unique surface state, resulting in a unique PL property (from blue to blue-green) [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of D-mannose-derived CDs in improvement of mung bean sprout growth
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8c shows the possible mechanism by which D-mannose-derived CDs promote mung bean growth. The germination, growth and metabolism of plants are related to factors such as photosynthesis and chlorophyll content[75].

Carbon dots, with their excellent biocompatibility, photoluminescent properties, ease of surface functionalization, and availability of a wide range of low-cost sources. It plays an active role in agricultural production[76,77]. From an optical point of view, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs matches the absorption spectrum of plant photosynthesis, which means that they are able to efficiently use the sun's ultraviolet rays and convert them into visible light that plants need, thus improving the photosynthesis process[78]. From the perspective of chemical structure, the hydroxyl, carboxyl and ester functional groups enriched on the surface of CDs can regulate the physiological and biochemical reactions in plants and improve the growth rate and yield of plants[79]. These help the growth and development of plants [30,35].

Fig. 7f shows that D-mannose-derived CDs can indeed promote the growth and development of mung bean sprouts, and the increase in chlorophyll content in mung bean sprouts indicates that the obtained CDs can promote its light absorption and photosynthesis, and promote the growth of roots、stem and leaves at the same time (as shown in Fig. 7a-7c), this statement was also confirmed by laser confocal images of mung bean seedlings (see Additional file Fig. S3). In addition, plant growth can often be promoted by absorbing water and nutrients. The CDs synthesized in this study can be easily absorbed on the surface of roots to promote root growth and enhance the absorption capacity of plants to water and nutrients, because they are small in size (the average particle size of GCDs and GLCDs is 2.63 nm and 1.22 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2), and have good water solubility.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5, both CDs have unique optical properties, which can absorb ultraviolet and visible light and convert the energy into a spectral range beneficial to plant photosynthesis (GCDs and GLCDs emit blue and blue-green light, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5a and 5b, Fig. S4 shows the chlorophyll absorption spectrum range).). This unique light conversion characteristic helps to improve the capture and utilization of light energy by mung beans, thereby enhancing the efficiency of photosynthesis [80]. It is also confirmed by the determination of chlorophyll content in mung bean sprouts in this study (Fig. 7). The CDs may promote chlorophyll synthesis by providing additional light energy or acting as an electron donor, increasing the chlorophyll content of plants and the photosynthesis rate.
Overall, a conclusion can be made that the obtained GCDs and GLCDs in this study can promote the growth of mung beans by improving root growth and increasing chlorophyll content.

This study successfully synthesized D-mannose-derived carbon dots (GCDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (GLCDs), and their physicochemical properties were comprehensively analyzed through a series of characterization techniques. The experimental results showed that GCDs and GLCDs had excellent optical properties and biocompatibility and could effectively promote the growth of mung bean sprouts. The introduction of nitrogen doping not only enhances the fluorescence properties of carbon dots but also may promote plant growth by increasing chlorophyll content and photosynthesis efficiency. In addition, the surface functional groups and nitrogen doping of carbon dots have essential effects on their optical properties and biological activity. Therefore, GCDs and GLCDs show excellent application prospects in the agricultural field, especially in improving crop yield and quality. Future research will further explore the mechanism of action of carbon dots in different plant growth stages and their potential application in practical agricultural production. At the same time, this study also pointed out the effect of carbon dots concentration on plant growth and found that the appropriate concentration of carbon dots solution can promote plant growth. In contrast, excessive concentration may have an inhibitory effect, which provides an essential reference for the dose control of carbon dots in agricultural applications.
